title: When NOT to use a home herbal remedy
subtitle: The red-flag list every reader should know first
excerpt: Six situations where a home herbal remedy is the wrong tool — and the action to take instead. The first article every reader in the herbal section should read.
sourceNotes: The principles draw on NHS 111 triage criteria (open government data, 2024-2026 versions), British Red Cross first-aid public-domain guidance, EMA HMPC monograph safety summaries, and the conservative voice of the western herbal tradition's modern practitioners (the NIMH and the AHG public guidance on when self-treatment is the wrong call). No single source carries the article; the framing is the publication's own.

content.text: The herbal section of this library is a slow-living, kitchen-table resource. The recipes are for minor everyday complaints — indigestion after a heavy meal, a tickling cough, a tension headache, a tired evening, dry skin in winter. These are the conditions that home herbal medicine has handled in European kitchens for centuries; they're also the conditions that resolve on their own with a bit of help.

What the library doesn't do is replace medical care. Some situations need a doctor, a nurse, a midwife, or an ambulance — and reaching for a remedy in those situations doesn't just delay treatment; it can make things worse. This article is a short red-flag list every reader in the herbal section should know first. It will be linked from every remedy page, and the publication recommends reading it once, properly, before any other herbal entry.

Not medical advice. Consult a qualified herbalist or doctor for ongoing or serious symptoms. The phone numbers below are UK ones; readers in other countries should use their local emergency number (911 in North America, 112 across most of Europe and Australia, 000 in Australia) and their local triage line.

attrs.title: The short version
attrs.body: If a situation is urgent (severe pain, breathlessness, suspected stroke, severe allergic reaction, severe burn, deep wound, fever in an infant, anything in a child that worries you, anything in a pregnant person that worries you, anything that's getting worse fast) — herbs are not the tool. Call 999 (UK) or your country's emergency number. Get help on the way before you make a tea.

content.text: 1. Anything urgent
Some symptoms are emergencies and need 999 (UK) or your country's emergency number, not a remedy.

Chest pain that radiates to the arm, jaw, or back; chest pain with sweating or breathlessness; chest pain that comes on with exertion. Possible heart attack. Call 999, sit down, and chew a 300 mg aspirin if you have one and you're not allergic to it.

Sudden severe breathlessness, especially with bluish lips or sucked-in ribs as you breathe. Possible severe asthma attack, anaphylaxis, pulmonary embolism, or pneumonia. Call 999. Sit upright, leaning slightly forward.

Stroke signs — face drooping on one side, arm weakness, speech slurred or muddled. The acronym is FAST (Face, Arms, Speech, Time). Call 999 immediately; the treatment window for stroke is short and counts in minutes.
Sudden severe headache, especially "the worst headache of my life", with stiff neck or fever, or after a head injury. Possible subarachnoid haemorrhage or meningitis. Call 999.

Severe allergic reaction — swollen lips or tongue, difficulty breathing, dizziness, sudden generalised rash. Anaphylaxis. Call 999; use an EpiPen if you have one; lie flat with legs raised unless breathing makes that worse.

Severe burns — any burn larger than the size of the casualty's palm, any burn on face, hands, feet, joints, or genitals, any burn that goes through the skin layer (white, leathery, charred), any chemical or electrical burn. Run cool running water over the burn for at least twenty minutes, then call 999 or get to A&E. No salve, no honey, no toothpaste, no butter.

Deep wounds — cuts that will not stop bleeding after fifteen minutes of firm pressure, cuts over a joint, cuts on the face or hand with possible nerve or tendon involvement, animal or human bites. A&E, not a home salve.

Suspected poisoning, overdose, or ingestion of anything dangerous. UK: National Poisons Information Service via 111. Don't try to make the person vomit; don't give them anything to drink unless directed by the service.
2. Anything in someone who is unwell

Fever and feeling rotten is one thing; fever with serious extra signs is another. A doctor's pair of eyes should look at any unwell person who has any of the following, before any herbal preparation goes near them.
Fever above 39°C in adults that doesn't respond to over-the-counter paracetamol or ibuprofen, or any fever lasting more than three days.

Fever in any infant under three months — this is a same-day A&E or 999 situation in the UK, regardless of how the baby looks. The window for diagnosing serious infection in tiny babies is short and the threshold is very low. Don't reach for a chamomile bath.
Fever with stiff neck, light sensitivity, or a non-blanching rash (a rash that doesn't fade when you press a glass against it). Possible meningitis. Call 999.
Any rash in an unwell person — call 111 for advice or your GP same-day. The herbalist's compress is not the right first response to a rash you haven't explained.

Any change in a baby's behaviour that worries you — unusual drowsiness, unusual irritability, refusing feeds, fewer wet nappies, high-pitched cry. Babies don't have other ways to tell you something's wrong. Trust the worry; call 111 or your GP same-day.
3. Anything in pregnancy or breastfeeding without specialist guidance

Herbal preparations cross to the baby through the cord blood in pregnancy and through breastmilk during breastfeeding. The dose the mother takes is not the dose the baby gets — but it isn't zero either. The publication's herbal preparations are written to be cautious here; the safety blocks on individual remedies name the herbs that pregnancy excludes and the herbs that need stage-specific advice.

But the harder line: any new herbal preparation in pregnancy is a conversation with your midwife rather than a kitchen experiment. The midwife knows your pregnancy; the library knows the herb. The two conversations need to meet.
Same for breastfeeding. The cup of chamomile that calms a wound-up evening is fine; the strong daily tincture is a herbalist conversation. The default position is to clear new preparations first.
4. Anything in children under twelve without paediatric herbal guidance

Paediatric dosing is not adult-divided-by-half. Some herbs are perfectly fine for children at a documented stage-specific dose; some are contraindicated regardless of dose; some need a different preparation form entirely (alcohol-free glycerites or syrups rather than tinctures).

Standard-strength teas of chamomile, fennel, lemon balm, and lavender from school age (5+) are the herbal practice that western herbal tradition records as widely tolerated, one cup in the evening or after a meal. Beyond that — anything more concentrated, anything for an infant, anything daily for more than a week — consult a paediatric herbalist or your GP. The library has the herbs; your paediatric herbalist or GP has the child.
5. Anything that doesn't get better, or gets worse

Home herbal remedies are for short-term, self-limiting complaints. A cold should turn the corner in a week. A tension headache should ease in a day or two. Indigestion after a heavy meal should settle in the evening. Mild skin irritation should respond to a few days of salve.

If the complaint doesn't follow that pattern — if a cold drags past ten days or worsens after initially improving, if a headache won't shift or comes back day after day, if indigestion is daily or keeps you awake at night, if a skin patch spreads or weeps or doesn't heal — the home remedy isn't the right tool any more. See your GP.
Daily reach-for-the-cup hides the question the doctor needs you to ask. Chronic symptoms have causes worth diagnosing.
6. Anything you're not sure about

The shortest rule. If you're unsure — about whether the herb is right, about whether the symptom is really minor, about whether your medication interacts, about whether the patch on the leg is the kind a salve treats — pause and ask. NHS 111 (UK) takes triage calls 24 hours a day; pharmacists are excellent for medication-interaction questions; your GP for anything that needs an examination; a registered herbalist (NIMH or AHG in the UK and US) for herbal-specific questions.

The herbal kitchen is one part of a household's medicine. The doctor's surgery, the pharmacy, the NHS 111 line, the practising herbalist — they're the rest. The library reaches for them often, and the publication's voice is calmer for that.
